name: Krasue
alias: Ahp · Kasu · the floating head that hunts in the dark
body: First there is a glow low over the paddy, soft and red like a lantern carried by no hand, drifting steadily against the wind when every honest light should bend with it. Then the smell finds you before the shape does, a wet reek of blood and offal and rot that no clean village should carry on a still night, the stink of a slaughter floor left too long in the heat. By day the woman next door is unremarkable, even lovely, polite at the market, slow to anger, generous with a smile over the vegetables. It is only after dark, when the rice fields exhale their cold mist and the dogs go quiet, that you might at last see the thing that travels above them: a human head, beautiful and lit from within as if a coal burned behind the face, its eyes a wet red, dragging beneath it the slick rope of its own organs, the heart and stomach and pale lungs still glistening and swaying as they trail through the night air.

The Krasue hunts whatever is soft and unguarded. She craves blood, the leavings of birth, raw flesh, carrion and filth, and so she haunts the places these are found, the cattle byre, the latrine pit, and above all the bed and floor of a woman in labour, where she lowers her trailing gut toward the newborn and the afterbirth. To recognise her is to grasp the bargain at her heart: by night the head tears free and flies out to feed while the body it abandoned lies hidden and helpless in some dark corner of the house, and the head must find that body and rejoin it before the first light, or be left to rot apart and die. Some say the curse runs in the blood, passed in silence from mother to daughter like an heirloom no one will name; some say it is the sentence of a sin in a former life, of sorcery worked or taboo flesh eaten, a debt the woman pays out nightly in the coin of her own glowing entrails.

What lingers is the cruelty of the everyday face, the certainty that the neighbour who smiled at your gate this morning may unspool herself at midnight into a thing that comes hunting your child. So the farmers of the northeast plant sharp thorned branches around the stilts of the house and beneath the floor of a birthing room, for entrails snagged and torn on thorns cannot knit whole again before sunrise, and a head that cannot rejoin its body is a head already dead. In the villages of Isan they still tell, low and certain, of a head found at first light tangled in the bamboo and the thorn, the mouth open over its stolen meal, the body it belonged to lying cold across the room and never breathing again.
note: Wards are practical and old: thorny branches (often bamboo or the thorns of citrus and sang khee) ringed around the house and under the birthing floor to snare the trailing organs, and the certain kill, finding the abandoned body by daylight and hiding, moving, or destroying it so the head cannot reattach before dawn. The Krasue is the Thai member of a wider Southeast Asian family of detaching female revenants: the Malay and Indonesian Penanggalan, the Cambodian Ahp (Krasue), the Lao Kasu or Phi Kasu, the Balinese Leyak, and the Philippine Manananggal. Across the region the shared logic holds: a woman by day, a flying viscera-trailing head by night, vulnerable in the moment of return to her own flesh.

The tale is told across Thailand and especially throughout Isan (the northeast), as well as in Laos and Cambodia, and remains a staple of regional horror cinema. Folk explanations range from inherited curse to karmic punishment for sorcery or for eating taboo food while alive.

recountings.text: In October 2023 villagers across the Mueang and Tha Wung districts of Lopburi province spread alarms on social media that they had encountered a Krasue, triggering a wave of fear through the community. Local authorities later debunked the scare, revealing the phantom was a chicken thief who had disguised himself with a mask of an elderly woman, faded straggly hair, and a set of fangs. A Saraburi resort owner had even offered a one million baht reward before withdrawing it over safety concerns. This is a specific documented news event.

Wikipedia records a string of widely circulated modern Krasue scares in Thailand, including 2008 CCTV footage from a Pathum Thani factory, mysterious floating lights reported in Ayutthaya province in 2014, and further sightings around 2023 in Lopburi and Ratchaburi provinces tied to glowing figures and unexplained livestock deaths. These are recurring rural panics passed around in news and social media rather than any one person's verified encounter.

A standard folkloric retelling collected by the Thailand culture site Sirinya's Thailand describes the Phi Krasue as an ordinary-looking woman by day who at night detaches her glowing red-eyed head, trailing her entrails, and flies off to feed on the raw flesh, blood, and organs of the living, leaving her headless body hidden in the dark. The legend frames her as a woman cursed for practicing black magic. This is broadly shared traditional lore, not a personal account.

references.title: Village Terrorized by Ghost: Unmasking the True Identity of the Dreaded Krasue
Unveiling Thailand's Dark Phi Krasue Folklore
A Floating Head with Dangling Entrails, the Southeast Asian Krasue
Krasue: Thailand's Floating Head Spirit of Horror and Hunger
